data.title: Crude oil ticks lower in slow post-holiday trade as Ukraine peace talks in focus
data.description:
Oil futures reversed late Friday to finish lower in thin post-Thanksgiving trading, as the market continued to watch for progress in Russia-Ukraine peace efforts that could eventually see U.S. sanctions lifted, while OPEC+ is expected to confirm at its weekend meeting that it will keep production targets steady in January.

"The fundamental outlook remains fairly similar to where it was at the group's last meeting," ING analysts said in a note. "Admittedly, though, there's more uncertainty over Russian oil supply."

The Energy Information Administration reported Friday that U.S. oil production rose to a record high in September, rising 44,000 bbl/day to a 13.84 million bbl/day.

Also, The New York Times reported late in the session that President Trump and Venezuela's President Maduro discussed a potential meeting in a call last week; a de-escalation of tensions between the U.S. and oil-rich Venezuela would remove another risk premium from oil prices.

Friday's session was marked by thin holiday volumes and an hours-long outage on Chicago Mercantile Exchange’s trading platform that roiled global markets.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for January delivery and front-month January Brent crude (CO1:COM) both closed -0.2% on Friday at $58.55/bbl and $63.20/bbl, respectively.

Despite rising ~1% for the week, both contracts fell for the fourth straight month, their longest losing streaks since 2023, as expectations for higher global supply weighed on prices.

However, U.S. natural gas futures (NG1:COM) rose for a sixth consecutive week to a nearly three-year high, as market fundamentals tightened through the holiday period with colder weather forecasts and rising heating demand; front-month Nymex natural gas for January delivery settled +6.4% on Friday to $4.850/MMBtu, the best settlement since December 27, 2022.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

Energy stocks, as represented by the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLE), finished the holiday-shortened week +1.1%.

Top 10 gainers in energy and natural resources in the past 5 days: New Era Energy & Digital (NUAI) +52.8%, T1 Energy (TE) +51.5%, Foremost Clean Energy (FMST) +39.4%, TMC the metals company (TMC) +33.6%, Snow Lake Resources (LITM) +32.5%, Canadian Solar (CSIQ) +26.7%, Fluence Energy (FLNC) +26.6%, VivoPower International (VVPR) +26.4%, PrimeEnergy Resources (PNRG) +25.5%, Stem (STEM) +24.9%.

Top 5 decliners in energy and natural resources in the past 5 days: Houston American Energy (HUSA) -37.7%, Geospace Technologies (GEOS) -23.8%, Imperial Petroleum (IMPP) -19%, Verde Clean Fuels (VGAS) -11.2%, OMS Energy Technologies (OMSE) -7.6%.

Source: Barchart.com

data.title: Petrobras cost cutting could reduce exploratory wells in Equatorial Margin, CFO says
data.description:
Petrobras (PBR) could review some of the 15 wells it plans to drill in the Equatorial Margin region, with crude oil prices expected to remain weak in the coming years, CEO Magda Chambriard said Friday.

As part of its new five-year investment plan for 2025-29, which lowers capital spending by ~2% to $109 billion from the previous plan for $111 billion to $109 billion, Petrobras (PBR) cut planned investments for the Equatorial Margin - considered the company's most promising oil frontier - which extends along Brazil's northern coastline from the state of Rio Grande do Norte to the state of Amapa by $500 million to $2.5 billion.

"We had a large set of wells for the Equatorial Margin; some were prioritized, others were, let's say, deprioritized depending on the price of Brent crude oil," Magda Chambriard said at a press conference Friday, according to Reuters.

Despite the capex cuts, Petrobras (PBR) expects to maintain its oil production at 2.6 million-2.7 million bbl/day until 2034 after ramping it up around 2027, Chambriard said.

But the company's cuts will affect extraordinary dividends to shareholders, CFO Fernando Melgarejo said, with a low possibility of doling out extra cash in the coming years.

data.title: Gold futures post fifth straight daily gain, silver surges to fresh record high
data.description:
Gold futures climbed Friday to their fifth consecutive daily gain to close at a more-than-two-week high, and silver surged to a new record, topping a peak set during last month's squeeze in the London market.

Gold and silver have been supported by rising hopes of a Federal Reserve interest rate cut in December, goosed by dovish commentary from Federal Reserve officials that seemed to raise the odds that policymakers will decide to reduce rates by at least 25 basis points next month, as well as ongoing tight supply and inflows into ETFs.

"The expectation is that we're going to continue to have a slower economy going into 2026, and the Federal Reserve is very likely to cut rates, which is getting some investors back" into gold, TD Securities global head of commodity strategy Bart Melek said in a note.

President Trump's comments this week that he would like to end the income tax, citing revenue expected from his tariff policy, is "very inflationary, and that's what gold is really responding to," according to Marketgauge.com chief strategist Michele Schneider, adding that $4,700/oz would make "a good next target" gold price forecast for 2026.

Friday's surge in silver also was sparked by lithium liquidity and erratic price moves following an hours-long halt to futures trading on the Comex exchange.

Chinese silver inventories reportedly have dropped sharply to their lowest level in 10 years, following a surge in Chinese silver exports in October to an all-time high of more than 660 metric tons in October that were triggered by a supply squeeze that pushed prices to record highs.

Silver has skyrocketed 95% YTD, as investors pile into alternative assets in a wider retreat from government bonds and currencies, and with the market expecting to see a fifth consecutive silver supply deficit this year.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for December delivery ended +1.3% to $4,218.30/oz, and front-month Comex December silver (XAGUSD:CUR) finished +6.7% to $56.44/oz; for the week, gold gained 3.4% and silver rallied 13.1%.

For November, gold rose 5.9% for its foruth consecutive monthly gain, and silver posted its seventh straight monthly gain, soaring 17.6%.

ETFs: (GLD), (GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)g

data.title: Stocks that dipped the most in November are ‘grotesquely over-valued’ – David Bahnsen
data.description:
David Bahnsen, American portfolio manager and founder of The Bahnsen Group, cautioned investors against blindly buying dips in overvalued technology stocks (XLK), despite the 5% market pullback observed in November.

“I think that the names that have dipped the most are grotesquely over-valued, and they could easily catch a bid and rally, but it’s entirely speculative,” said Bahnsen.

He specifically highlighted Nvidia (NVDA), noting the stock experienced a nearly 20% drawdown mid-month “on really, really good news, not bad news,” suggesting the decline was driven by the stock’s expensive valuation rather than disappointing performance.

In addition, portfolio managers are facing unprecedented challenges as 2025 comes to a close, according to Michael Farr, CEO and founder of investment advisory firm Farr, Miller, and Washington.

He described the current environment as “hard to manage at this year-end as any time you can remember.”

The difficulties stem from concentrated positions that have significantly increased in value over the year, forcing managers to balance risk management with tax consequences while identifying genuine value in the market, he said.

For investors seeking opportunities, Bahnsen recommended rotating into “better value sides of the market,” noting that while Bitcoin (BTC-USD) and AI-related stocks (ARTY), (AIQ), (AIEQ) struggled in November, healthcare (XLK) rose by double digits and consumer staples (XLP) gained approximately 800 basis points relative to the S&P 500 (SP500).

“Not to get out of risk, but whether it’s dividend growth, value-type things that we like, or growth at a reasonable price, that’s all different than buying things at 60x earnings you hope go to 80x earnings,” Bahnsen said.

Farr, however, warned against being “overly cautious,” particularly regarding stocks like Nvidia (NVDA) that continue to deliver strong performance.

“They’ve got a half trillion dollars in orders on chips for 2025 and 2026 combined. They are delivering numbers,” Farr noted, while acknowledging the valuation concerns.

He also identified potential opportunities in healthcare (XLV), energy (XLE), and consumer durables heading into 2026.

data.title: Wall Street posts largest swing ever from red to green for the month of November
data.description:
Wall Street's S&P 500 index (SP500) on Black Friday turned positive for November, marking an impressive rebound for the benchmark gauge from lows of as much as nearly 5% a week ago. The reversal is the largest swing from red to green in the month of November ever.

The S&P on November 20 closed at a monthly low of 6,538.76 points, and at that point was down -4.4% for the month. On November 21, it hit a session low at 6,521.92 points, meaning it had fallen as much as -4.7% for the month. The recovery began that day itself, with the S&P ending that session higher to kick off a five-day win streak culminating on Black Friday. The benchmark index is now +0.1% for the month 

That 4.5 percentage point swing from November 20's closing level to Black Friday's is the largest ever for any November, Carson Group chief market strategist Ryan Detrick noted on X.

Equities came under intense pressure mid-November as the artificial intelligence trade broke down and rate cut odds faded amid hawkish commentary from Federal Reserve speakers. Coming off from a hot October that saw Wall Street notch multiple record highs, stocks were also primed for a bout of profit booking.

But the mood has swiftly improved over the last few days, chiefly due to economic data that has supported an interest rate cut in December. Historical patterns of trading have also played a part, with Carson's Detrick pointing out earlier this week that markets tend to bottom on November 20 before the year-end Santa rally. 

"Stocks have erased November’s losses during today’s shortened trading session, as confidence of a December rate cut coincide with favorable seasonals for markets," José Torres, senior economist at Interactive Brokers, said on Friday. 

"The reopening of the government additionally boosted optimism of an economic reacceleration in 2026, while strong results on the corporate earnings front helped bulls keep AI doubters and valuation hawks alike at bay while the technical damage in the charts was repaired," he said.

"The most significant risk to the cycle at this juncture is rising unemployment but subdued jobless claims alongside an expectation that monetary policy accommodation will generate increased hiring is supporting sentiment," Torres added.

With the S&P 500 (SP500) having turned positive for the month, see the top and bottom 10 performers in the benchmark index in terms of their November stock performance:

data.title: Algoma Steel surges after Canada unveils more help for tariff-hit steel industry
data.description:
Algoma Steel (ASTL) closed +9.5% on Friday after Prime Minister Carney announced steps Wednesday to provide more support to help Canada's steel and lumber industries cope with U.S. tariffs and create a domestic market.

The federal government will reduce the quota for steel imports from countries that do not have a free trade agreement with Canada to 20% from 50% of 2024 levels, and countries with an FTA with Canada will see their quotas cut to 75% from 100% of the 2024 level; the U.S. and Mexico, which are bound by the United States-Canada-Mexico free trade deal, are not included.

The government also will cut freight rates to ship steel and lumber across Canada by 50%, starting next spring, Carney said.

Canadian efforts aimed at supporting the country’s steel industry should be positive for Algoma Steel (ASTL), although the extent to which the measures support profitable steel pricing in Canada remains uncertain, RBC Capital analyst James McGarragle said.

The Carney government believes the measures will unlock more than C$1 billion in new domestic steel demand, which compares with the consensus 2025 revenue forecast for Algoma (ASTL) of C$2.1 billion, and McGarragle said taking the numbers at face value and assuming Algoma can capture 25% of the upside - roughly its percentage of Canadian capacity - implies solid upside potential to next year’s estimates.

RBC has a Sector Perform rating and C$5.46 target on Algoma (ASTL) shares.

data.title: Maris-Tech secures $2 million financing
data.description:
• Maris-Tech (MTEK) announced on Friday that it has entered into a note purchase agreements, pursuant to which, the company issued non-interest bearing convertible promissory notes in the aggregate principal amount of $2 million.
  • The company intends to use the net proceeds for working capital and general corporate purposes, and to support initiatives intended to strengthen its capital structure and its U.S. commercial market activities.
  • The notes are convertible into ordinary shares of the company pursuant to their terms, which include set conversion periods and a conversion price floor. One note provides for an initial conversion window beginning six months after issuance, followed by full convertibility beginning twelve months after issuance. The other Note becomes fully convertible beginning twelve months after issuance, the company said. 
  • MTEK +8.20% after hours to $1.45.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: SA Asks: Will the Fed cut interest rates in December?
data.description:
Will the Federal Reserve's Federal Open Market Committee vote to lower interest rates at next month's meeting, which is scheduled for Dec. 9-10?

Seeking Alpha analysts Jeremy LaKosh, Jack Bowman, and Hawinvest weigh in. 

Jeremy LaKosh: I believe the Fed will cut rates at the December meeting and potentially project a hold on future rate cuts through 2026. The Fed made it clear over the summer that it valued protecting the labor market in the short term over getting to its 2% inflation target. With unemployment rising in line with the Fed's expectations, there's no reason they should deviate from the path they projected in September.

Jack Bowman: The Fed is currently stuck between a rock and a hard place with rising inflation and unemployment. They've indicated that they're likely to prioritize unemployment at past meetings, and so I'm inclined to think that with an unemployment rate now at 4.4% and no October jobs report (canceled) to show a downtrend or deceleration, we will see a 25 bp cut at the next meeting. I think we'll likely see a lot of hawkish talk out of the Fed while they cut, and we may see a pause going into next year. 

At the last meeting, they used the term "risk management cut." I imagine we'll hear that again. I think this will signal to investors that long rates are due to come down over time. So intermediate treasuries (IEF) and long treasuries (TLT) could benefit here while the market reacts to the Fed cut, as the bond market prices in lower longer rates as well as the lower short rate set by the Fed.

Hawinvest: Yes, the Fed will cut rates in December because the risk of not doing so is too great with unemployment ticking higher and 2025 being one of the worst years for layoffs since the Great Financial Crisis. The economy is slowing, which should alleviate the Fed's inflation concerns, and AI-related job losses are likely to accelerate in 2026, so keeping a slightly restrictive rate policy no longer makes sense. Plus, we are only talking about a 1/4 point rate cut, which is not a big risk for the Fed to take.

In anticipation of the rate cut, I think it makes sense to buy interest rate-sensitive and beaten-down stocks like Whirlpool (WHR), which David Tepper recently purchased a very large stake in, and Home Depot (HD).

data.title: RCI names Reese as interim President and CEO and Molina as interim CFO
data.description:
• RCI Hospitality (NASDAQ:RICK) announced on Friday that it named Travis Reese as interim President and CEO and Albert Molina as interim CFO.
  • Reese and Molina will be succeeding Eric Langan and Bradley Chhay.
  • Reese has been instrumental in the development and growth of the company.
  •  Molina has been Director of financial reporting since joining.
  • Langan will continue to serve on the Board, giving his advice on strategic corporate transactions, identifying acquisition targets, and negotiating potential transactions.
  • Similarly, Chhay will continue advising on financial budgeting and forecasting, capital allocation strategy, mergers and acquisitions, and related integration.

data.title: Carrier Connect Data Solutions acquires Purecolo
data.description:
• Carrier Connect Data Solutions (CCDS:CA)  announced on Friday that it had entered into a definitive share purchase agreement on November 27, 2025, to acquire all the issued and outstanding securities of PureColo.
  • According to the agreement, the company will issue 4.60M common shares and a cash consideration of approximately $2.33M to the PureColo security holders in connection with the acquisition.
  • Cash consideration will be paid over a span of 9 months, subject to certain debt covenants of PureColo in the agreement.
  • The acquisition will expand the company geographically with the addition of 4 data centers in 3 regions.
  • Shares +8.75%.
